critique.summary: The UK regulator Ofcom fined the US messaging platform 4Chan £520,000 for breaches of the Online Safety Act, including failure to implement age checks and risk assessments. 4Chan has mocked the fine and stated it will not pay, arguing that its conduct is legal in the US and protected by the First Amendment.
mainPoints: Ofcom fined 4Chan £520,000 for failing to comply with the UK's Online Safety Act.
The fines include £450,000 for lack of age checks to prevent children from accessing pornography.
Additional fines of £50,000 and £20,000 were imposed for failing to assess risks and protect users from criminal content.
4Chan's lawyer responded with an AI-generated cartoon and stated the platform is not breaking US law.
Ofcom emphasized the importance of protecting children online and enforcing age checks and risk assessments.
4Chan has refused to pay previous fines from Ofcom.
Ofcom has issued nearly £3 million in fines to various tech companies under the Online Safety Act, with limited payment compliance.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are consistent with publicly available information about Ofcom's enforcement of the UK's Online Safety Act and the fines issued to 4Chan. The amounts and reasons for the fines align with official statements from Ofcom. The response from 4Chan's lawyer and the platform's stance on the fines have been reported by multiple reputable sources. The article also accurately reflects Ofcom's broader enforcement actions and challenges in collecting fines. Overall, the article is factually accurate and truthful.
considerations: The article does not explore the legal complexities of jurisdiction and enforcement of UK laws on US-based companies like 4Chan.
There is no detailed discussion of the effectiveness of the Online Safety Act or potential impacts on free speech.
The article does not provide perspectives from 4Chan users or independent experts on online safety regulations.
The long-term implications for international tech companies facing UK regulations are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the UK Ofcom's attempt to fine 4chan for online safety breaches, with commenters debating jurisdictional authority, free speech, and the practicality of enforcing such fines on foreign companies. Some highlight similar international cases, others criticize the UK's approach as overreach or censorship, while a few comment on 4chan's cultural impact and legal responses.

comments.author: EmbarrassedHelp
comments.text:
Ofcom is currently threatening a Canadian forum that exists to help people with depression. Ofcom claims that geoblocking blocking the UK is "insufficient":

> I've also gone back to Ofcom explicitly telling them the UK was now geoblocked (twice now) and I received a response that this was insufficient.

Source: https://www.reddit.com/r/LegalAdviceUK/comments/1rk690v/i_ru...

Ofcom really thinks that their laws apply globally.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a concrete example of Ofcom's expansive jurisdictional claims, supported by a source, illustrating the broader issue of regulatory overreach.

comments.author: miohtama
comments.text:
Germany tried earlier to fine American companies for online posts using a law called "NetzDG".

Gab refused to pay the fine, and it was over.

> The enforcement notice itself highlights the structural tension. Despite acknowledging Gab’s US address, the German government asserts authority to pursue collection, including formal enforcement proceedings, without identifying any German subsidiary or office.

> The payment instructions route funds directly to the German federal treasury, showing that the action is punitive rather than remedial.

> Germany’s approach also reveals the paper trail behind modern censorship enforcement. The fine stems not from a specific post or statement, but from alleged failure to comply with aspects of NetzDG. That procedural hook enables broader regulatory reach, transforming administrative requirements into a mechanism for speech governance.

https://reclaimthenet.org/gab-refuses-to-pay-germanys-fine-c...
comments.analysis: This comment contextualizes the UK situation with a similar German case, highlighting the difficulties in enforcing foreign fines on US-based companies.

comments.author: OsrsNeedsf2P
comments.text:
4chan's lawyer's response:

"In the only country in which 4chan operates, the United States, it is breaking no law and indeed its conduct is expressly protected by the First Amendment."[0]

[0] https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c624330lg1ko
comments.analysis: This accurately reflects the legal defense based on jurisdiction and US free speech protections, a central point in the dispute.

comments.author: dijit
comments.text:
The response from Ofcom doesn’t stand up to scrutiny.

If you are to sell a toy in the UK you must be a British company. (and must pay VAT and comply with British safety standards).

If a consumer buys from overseas and imports a product then they do not have British consumer protections. Which is why so much aliexpress electrical stuff is dangerous (expecially USB chargers) yet it continues to be legally imported.

Just, no british retailer would be allowed to carry it without getting a fine.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly explains UK consumer law distinctions, providing useful context for understanding the regulatory environment.

comments.author: veunes
comments.text: 520k sounds like a lot until you realize it's basically unenforceable if the company just ignores it
comments.analysis: This practical observation about enforcement challenges is accurate and relevant to the discussion of regulatory impact.

comments.author: 4ggr0
comments.text:
i love the cognitive dissonance of some USians in this comment section who believe in both of these statements:

"4chan is a US company operating in the US, sure it serves content to global users but the jurisdiction is the US, we have free speech, ..."

"Sure, Company X is operating in Europe, but it also serves US users so it has to respect our laws and it's warranted for the US to apply pressure and fines."

at least decide for one side of the argument instead of just going the blind patriot way.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully points out inconsistencies in jurisdictional arguments made by some participants.

comments.author: DroneBetter
comments.text:
> Last month Pornhub restricted access to its website in the UK, blaming the introduction of stricter age checks, and said its traffic had fallen by 77%.

assumedly the rate of consumption hasn't dramatically changed, so the OSA's immediate result has been either the decentralisation of porn providers (towards those small enough to dodge the law for now and be less exacting) or the mass adoption of proxies; I assume the former is the path of least resistance

this is notably the opposite of the feared outcome (which I suspect may be closer to the long-term effect) that the bar to meet the requirements would be so high (possibly involving hiring a lawyer) that smaller social/porn sites get regulated out of existence (see ie. https://lobste.rs/s/ukosa1/uk_users_lobsters_needs_your_help...)
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view of regulatory effects on online content providers, supported by observed data.

comments.author: ecshafer
comments.text: UK fining an American company for this is absurd. 4Chan isn't breaking any laws. You can make it illegal for your own citizens but you can't regulate a foreign business. UK citizens should fight for the right to free speech though.
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional, this comment correctly highlights jurisdictional limits on national law enforcement.

comments.author: gorgoiler
comments.text:
Meanwhile Google.com shows all manner of depravity if you click “safe search: off”.

I realize there’s a carve out in the legislation for search engines but if the goal is to stop little Timmy finding pictures of an X being Yd up the Z then it is a resolute failure.

The only thing that works with children is transparency and accountability, be that the school firewall or a ban on screen use in secret.

”screens where I can see ‘em!”
comments.analysis: This comment critiques the effectiveness of content regulation, emphasizing the challenges of protecting children online.

comments.author: windowliker
comments.text:
4chan is already on most of the adult content filters used in the UK at ISP level (along with other such egregious offenders against childhood purity as archive.org) meaning that an explicit request to remove the filter is often required to access it... That it has taken this long to implement a universal block at the ISP level shows that the motive is something other than 'protecting the innocent'.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_blocking_in_the_United_Kin...
comments.analysis: This comment provides factual information about existing filtering practices, questioning the stated motives of regulators.
